nativeRange.habitat: Blackwater and whitewater floodplain rivers; slow-moving or still waters with surface vegetation

diet.notes: The Silver Arowana is a large predator that in captivity tends to accept large floating pellets, frozen krill, silversides, whole shrimp, mussels, chopped fish flesh, crickets, and earthworms. Feeder fish are generally discouraged due to the risk of introducing disease and their poor nutritional profile. Mammalian or avian meat should also be avoided. This species feeds primarily from the water surface, an adaptation reflected in its upward-angled mouth.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by user:Khomelka, 2390x1600. Sharp full-body side profile showing accurate metallic silver coloration with pink scale margins, ribbon-like dorsal/anal fins running the length of the body, upturned mouth and paired chin barbels - the classic display look.

Photo by Vaikoovery, 3357x1424. High-res dramatic full-length profile under aquarium lighting that brings out the pearlescent sheen; large scales, ribbon fins and barbels all clearly visible (pink cast is from tank lighting).
Photo by ShajiA, 5184x3456. Sharp whole-fish shot cruising just below the surface, illustrating this species' top-of-water swimming behaviour; ribbon fins and upturned mouth clearly shown.

summary: The Silver Arowana (Osteoglossum bicirrhosum) is a large Amazonian predator capable of reaching 90 cm and leaping up to 2 m clear of the water — a species suited only to dedicated keepers with purpose-built tanks of 2,000 L or more.
careNotes:
The Silver Arowana is widely considered unsuitable for the typical home aquarium, and many juveniles sold in the trade — commonly around 10 cm at purchase — fail to reach maturity in private collections due to inadequate tank size. Growth rate is rapid, roughly 30 cm per year in the first two years, so early planning for an adult-sized setup is essential.

Escape risk is extreme: the Silver Arowana is capable of leaping over 2 m clear of the water surface to catch prey and will readily jump if startled or if the tank lid has any gap. A heavy, tight-fitting cover with no openings is non-negotiable.

Adult specimens are highly territorial toward conspecifics and will consume any fish small enough to fit in their mouth, generally anything under approximately 30 cm. Community setups are possible only in very large tanks with suitably large, non-aggressive tankmates such as large catfish, Cichla cichlids, or pacus.

Drop eye syndrome — a permanent downward displacement of the eye — is commonly reported when arowanas are kept in tanks where they habitually look downward for food. Feeding from the surface and avoiding bottom-only feeding is generally advised to help reduce this risk. Tank decor should avoid sharp edges.

The Asian Arowana (Scleropages formosus) is a distinct species that is sometimes confused with the Silver Arowana. It is listed under CITES Appendix I and is prohibited in many countries including the USA. The Silver Arowana (Osteoglossum bicirrhosum) carries no such restriction but requires substantial custom infrastructure.

Water quality should be maintained with weekly large water changes of 30–50%. High dissolved oxygen and moderate flow are beneficial. The species is found in both blackwater and whitewater habitats in the wild and tolerates a wide pH range of 5.0–7.5.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; males carry eggs, larvae, and early juveniles in the mouth for approximately six weeks. Breeding in home aquaria has not been reliably documented; commercial production occurs in large outdoor ponds in Southeast Asia and South America. Spawning in the wild is seasonal (flood season, approximately December–January). Typical brood size is 12–30 fry, which measure 50–75mm at the time of release.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of Aquarium Co-Op (no species page found for Silver Arowana on that site after multiple URL attempts and site search).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, family, order, max size (90cm TL), and native range. Temperature stated as 24–30°C, aligning with SeriouslyFish upper range.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters, size, lifespan (10–15 years captive), tank size (300 gal / ~1136L — biased to 2000L per playbook large-predator rule), and care level. gH reported as 1–8 dGH vs SeriouslyFish 2–15 dGH; averaged to 2–12 dGH. Temperature 24–29°C consistent with consensus range used.

fish.breedingNotes: No confirmed home-aquarium breeding; commercial breeding occurs in large outdoor facilities. Paternal mouthbrooder with brood of 12–30 fry.
